Title: The wound-activated ERF15 transcription factor drives Marchantia polymorpha regeneration by activating an oxylipin biosynthesis feedback loop

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Its wound-induced expression is reduced in mperf15ko mutants, suggesting MpERF15 positively affects auxin biosynthesis-related response during regeneration
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR after wounding in CRISPR knockout lines
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Slow wound-responsive induction; reduced at 4 h after wounding in mperf15ko
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpASA1 is a downstream wound-responsive gene affected by MpERF15. 2. Its induction is reduced in mperf15ko mutants. 3. The paper suggests it may contribute to later auxin level restoration during regeneration.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core metabolism-relevant downstream gene experimentally tested in Results, but auxin metabolism is secondary to the paper’s main oxylipin focus.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts downstream of MpERF15 in wound-induced regeneration network; its wound inducibility is partially dependent on MpCOI1, MpAOC, and MpERF15
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR expression analysis after wounding and in overexpression lines
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Relatively late wound inducible (~6–12 h after wounding); elevated in MpERF15OE
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpERF20/LAXR is induced later than MpERF15 after wounding. 2. Its induction is partially dependent on MpERF15, MpCOI1, and MpAOC. 3. It likely operates downstream of the MpERF15-mediated oxylipin response during regeneration.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core regeneration gene in the network but not directly assigned to nutrient metabolite production.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes fatty-acid-derived allene oxide intermediates to form the cyclopentenone precursor leading to OPDA/dn-OPDA
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for direct biosynthesis of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of MpAOC reduces regeneration after wounding; exogenous OPDA rescues the regeneration defect
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout; regeneration assay after apical notch excision; RT-qPCR; OPDA rescue treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Wound inducible; transcript induction after tissue damage is reduced in Mperf15ko and Mpcoi1-2
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control step in this study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpAOC is a wound-inducible oxylipin biosynthesis gene. 2. Its wound-induced expression depends on MpERF15 and MpCOI1. 3. CRISPR mpaoc mutants show delayed regeneration that is rescued by OPDA, demonstrating that OPDA/dn-OPDA biosynthesis is essential for regeneration.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point to modulate wound-responsive oxylipin accumulation and regeneration capacity, but sustained activation may impair growth
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced regeneration when disrupted; excessive oxylipin pathway activation is associated with compact slow-growing thalli
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as genetic evidence that oxylipin accumulation is necessary for regeneration; authors describe MpAOC as performing a key step in OPDA/dn-OPDA biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies direct precursor for target-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced wound-induced expression in Mperf15ko indicates impaired OPDA/dn-OPDA biosynthesis under loss of MpERF15
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not different under unwounded conditions; wound inducible in WT; induction is significantly lower in Mperf15ko
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpAOS2 is an MpERF15-dependent wound-inducible oxylipin biosynthesis gene. 2. Its transcript accumulation after tissue damage is reduced in Mperf15ko. 3. It is up-regulated in MpERF15 overexpression lines in an MpCOI1-dependent manner.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate enzyme gene for engineering oxylipin-mediated wound responses and regeneration
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely growth inhibition if pathway is constitutively overactivated as seen in MpERF15OE background
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct mutant analysis reported for MpAOS2 in this paper.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AP2/ERF family, ERF subfamily class X
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Oxylipin biosynthesis feedback regulation during wound response and regeneration
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation of oxylipin biosynthesis genes within a positive feedback loop; also COI1-dependent self-amplification
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MpAOS2; MpAOC; endogenous MpERF15; MpERF20/LAXR (genetically upstream/positive effect suggested); MpASA1
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates wound-induced expression of MpAOS2 and MpAOC; promotes MpASA1 expression at later stages after wounding; increases MpERF20/LAXR expression
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Wounding; OPDA treatment; MpCOI1 signaling; likely MpMYC-dependent signaling inferred from cited microarray
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Associates with JAZ repressors based on yeast two-hybrid
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential positive regulator of wound-induced oxylipin accumulation and regeneration; knockout delays regeneration and lowers OPDA/dn-OPDA, whereas overexpression increases dn-OPDA, cell proliferation, and apical notch number
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; CRISPR-Cas9 knockout; constitutive overexpression; inducible overexpression; untargeted metabolomics; OPDA feeding; genetic interaction with Mpcoi1-2; yeast two-hybrid; cross-species complementation in Arabidopsis
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Instantly wound induced, >10-fold within 15 min, peaks at 1 h and declines thereafter; basal expression reduced in Mpcoi1-2; inducible by OPDA
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding; OPDA/dn-OPDA treatment; continuous white light growth conditions; dexamethasone in inducible lines
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key wound-activated regulator that both responds to and promotes oxylipin biosynthesis, forming an amplification loop essential for regeneration
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Positive feedback loop with MpCOI1 and OPDA/dn-OPDA; OPDA induces MpERF15, and MpERF15 promotes oxylipin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpERF15 is rapidly wound induced and required for gemmaling regeneration after tissue incision. 2. MpERF15 activates oxylipin biosynthesis genes MpAOS2 and MpAOC, promoting accumulation of OPDA/dn-OPDA. 3. MpERF15 functions downstream and upstream of MpCOI1 in a positive feedback loop. 4. Overexpression causes dn-OPDA accumulation, hyperproliferation, excess apical notches, and compact disorganized thalli, all largely MpCOI1 dependent. 5. MpERF15 can complement Arabidopsis erf109 regeneration defects, indicating functional conservation.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate regulator to enhance regeneration or wound responsiveness, but should be transiently or spatially controlled because constitutive activation causes severe growth defects
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive overexpression causes dwarfed, compact, disorganized thalli, slower growth, hypersensitivity to OPDA/dn-OPDA, increased cell number and reduced cell size
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core regulator and primary subject of the study; wound inducibility is MpCOI1 independent, but full expression level and downstream effects require MpCOI1.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: COI1 jasmonate receptor family; F-box receptor (standard assignment)
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Perception-dependent feedback control of oxylipin biosynthesis and regeneration
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Signal perception required for activation of MpERF15-dependent oxylipin biosynthesis and response outputs
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes basal and wound-associated MpERF15 expression and is required for MpERF15 overexpression to induce MpAOS2/MpAOC
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for regeneration and for MpERF15 overexpression phenotypes; mpcoi1-2 shows delayed regeneration and cannot be rescued by exogenous OPDA
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (Mpcoi1-2); RT-qPCR; regeneration assay; OPDA rescue assay; genetic interaction with MpERF15 overexpression
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression not dependent on MpERF15 under control or wound conditions
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential signaling node enabling the positive feedback loop that amplifies wound-induced oxylipin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Positive feedback with MpERF15 and OPDA/dn-OPDA
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MpCOI1 is required for proper regeneration after wounding. 2. MpERF15 expression is strongly reduced in mpcoi1-2, placing MpERF15 downstream of MpCOI1. 3. MpERF15 overexpression phenotypes and induction of MpAOS2/MpAOC are lost in the mpcoi1-2 background. 4. Exogenous OPDA rescues mperf15ko and mpaocko but not mpcoi1-2, showing receptor requirement for OPDA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to manipulate oxylipin sensitivity and regeneration competence, though broad hormone-signaling effects are likely
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function compromises regeneration and OPDA responsiveness
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although central to the feedback loop, it is a receptor/signaling component rather than a transcription factor or enzyme.
